---
title: "Reverse Engineering ESPN's Total Quarterback Rating Formula with Quantile Regression in Julia, R, and Python"
author:
  - name: Amara McGowan
    url: https://www.linkedin.com/in/amaramcgowan/
    affiliations:
      - ref: slu
  - name: Ivan Ramler
    email: mailto:iramler@stlawu.edu
    affiliations:
      - ref: slu
affiliations:
  - id: slu
    name: St. Lawrence University
date: "TBD"
description: "TBD"
format: html
categories:
  - Quantile Regression
---

Please note that these material have not yet completed the required
pedagogical and industry peer-reviews to become a published module on
the SCORE Network. However, instructors are still welcome to use these
materials if they are so inclined.

# Welcome Video

If you are unfamiliar with ESPN's Total Quarterback Rating (QBR), you can watch this video to learn more:

<iframe width="560" height="315" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/YaEixC8ieVg?si=Z5stXflgrJnU8iQD" title="YouTube video player" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen></iframe>

# Introduction 

The National Football League (NFL) has 14 official positions: 7 offensive, 5 defensive, and 2 special teams positions. One of the offensive positions is called Quarterback (QB), and their role is to lead their team on the field. ESPN streams NFL games and provides a lot of data on NFL players, including their Total Quarterback Rating (QBR), a 0-100 score measuring a quarterback's contribution to winning. Higher scores indicate better performance.

In this module, you will...

:::{.callout-note collapse="true" title="Learning Objectives" appearance="minimal"}
:::

:::{.callout-note collapse="true" title="Activity Length" appearance="minimal"}
:::

:::{.callout-note collapse="true" title="Methods" appearance="minimal"}
:::

# Data

The `nfl_qbr.csv` dataset contains 540 rows, with 65 quarterbacks... 

Download Data: [nfl_qbr.csv](nfl_qbr.csv)

<details>
<summary><b>Variable Descriptions</b></summary>

| Variable | Description |
|------------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| week | Week of the Season |
| player | Full Name of the Quarterback |
| qbr | What is it? |
| team | What is it? |
| opponent_team | What is it? |
| completions | What is it? |
| attempts | What is it? |
| passing_yards | What is it? |
| passing_tds | What is it? |
| passing_interceptions | What is it? |
| sacks | What is it? |
| fumbles | What is it? |
| prop_total | What is it? |
| result | What is it? |
</details>

<details>
<summary><b>Data Source</b></summary>

Include something about ESPN

</details>

# Quantile Regression

Here

# Getting Started in Julia, R, or Python

:::{.panel-tabset}

## Julia

Package: QuantReg.jl

```markdown
Julia code
```
## R

Package: quantreg package

```markdown
R code
```

## Python

Package: statsmodels

```markdown
Python code
```
:::

# References

Ho T, Carl S (2026). nflreadr: Download 'nflverse' Data. R package version 1.5.0.9002, https://nflreadr.nflverse.com.
